full_response:
Let R = {r1 , … , rn } be the n routers whose centres lie in the 4×4-square
      B = [0,4]×[0,4].
For i ≠ j let rij be the Euclidean distance between the centres of ri and rj .
A set S⊆R is feasible (“can be left on’’) iff rij ≥ 2 for every
ri , rj ∈ S.  Our goal is to find a feasible S of maximum size.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A geometric upper bound – there can never be more than 16 routers
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Partition B into sixteen 1×1 sub–squares  

        Bpq = [p ,p+1] × [q ,q+1]           for p,q ∈ {0,1,2,3} .

Lemma 1  
Every 1×1 sub–square contains the centre of at most one router of a
feasible set.

Proof.  
If two centres lie in the same Bpq their x– and y–coordinates differ
by < 1, hence the distance between them is < √1²+1² = √2 < 2,
contradicting feasibility. ∎

Corollary 2  
|S| ≤ 16 for every feasible S.  (At most one centre per 1×1
sub–square and there are only 16 such squares.)  This bound depends
only on the constant size of the library, not on n.

-------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Consequence of the bound – brute force becomes polynomial
-------------------------------------------------------------
Because an optimum solution never contains more than 16 routers we
can afford to enumerate all subsets of routers of size at most 16 and
keep the largest feasible one:

Algorithm MAX-ROUTERS(B,R)
    best ← ∅
    for k = 1 … 16
        for every k-element subset S of R      ⟨  C(n,k) possibilities ⟩
            if S is feasible                   ⟨  O(k²) ≤ 256 distance tests ⟩
                if |S| > |best|     then best ← S
    return best

Running time  
∑_{k=0}^{16} C(n,k) · O(k²) = O(n¹⁶)   (the exponent 16 is a
constant), i.e. polynomial in n.
The algorithm stores at most 16 router indices → O(n) space.

---------------------------------------------------------
3.  Correctness
---------------------------------------------------------
Soundness  
Every subset S examined by the algorithm is explicitly tested for the
pairwise-distance condition, therefore any S that is returned is
feasible.

Completeness  
By Corollary 2 an optimal solution S* contains at most 16 routers, so
S* appears among the subsets enumerated.  When S* is examined it is
feasible and its cardinality is at least as large as every other
feasible subset; hence best will be updated to have |best| = |S*|.
Consequently the algorithm outputs an optimal solution.

---------------------------------------------------------
4.  Optional speed-up (not required for polynomiality)
---------------------------------------------------------
Instead of enumerating all k-element subsets we can exploit the
1×1-grid directly: for each of the 16 squares choose either “no
router’’ or “one of the routers whose center lies in the square’’.
That is at most ∏_{cells c}(1+nc) ≤ (n+1)¹⁶ possibilities and is
often much faster in practice, but the worst-case order of growth is
the same O(n¹⁶).

---------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion
---------------------------------------------------------
Because any feasible set contains at most 16 routers, enumerating all
subsets of size ≤16 and keeping the largest feasible one gives a
correct algorithm whose running time is O(n¹⁶), i.e. polynomial in
the input size.  Hence the maximum number of routers that can remain
on inside the 4×4 library can indeed be determined in polynomial time.

\boxed{\text{The optimum can be found in }O(n^{16})\text{ time – polynomial because a feasible set contains }\le 16\text{ routers.}}

Confidence: 55%
extracted_answer: \text{The optimum can be found in }O(n^{16})\text{ time – polynomial because a feasible set contains }\le 16\text{ routers.}
